为什么大模型厂商都与投资机构成立合资公司？这意味着大模型在B端的竞争进入了地面阵地战模式。这两天大模型厂商最大的动作无疑是Authropic和OpenAI在同一天宣布联合PE投资机构、投行等成立AI-native 企业服务的合资公司（JV），堪称“AI大模型厂商在企业落地赛道上的正面交锋”。

Anthropic与Blackstone、Hellman & Friedman 以及 Goldman Sachs合作；而OpenAI则是拉上了TPG、Brookfield Asset Management、Bain Capital还有软银几家机构。
成立的合资公司都是企业服务性质的，将各自的大模型推广给广大中型企业，帮助它们将AI部署到核心业务运营中，两家大模型厂商会派驻工程师、顾问，提供从定制、集成、合规到持续优化的全套服务。

1、为什么要找这些投资机构合作？
1）这些投资机构手里握着成千上万家 portfolio company（覆盖医疗、制造、金融、零售等）。通过合作，大模型厂商能快速获取客户群，同时为这些 PE 公司带来生产力提升和成本节约（提升投资回报 IRR）。这是一种高效的分销策略，避免一家家推销。

2）AI 采用瓶颈在“部署”而非“模型”本身，模型再强，企业也缺“最后一公里”——集成、流程重构、合规、培训、持续迭代。传统咨询公司太贵太慢，大模型厂商又没有足够咨询人才。

跟投资机构成立合资公司，招募专业的部署、实施、培训人才，加快企业采用AI的进程和速度，同时加快自己在B端的拓展，抢夺更多的B端客户。这就是Authropic和OpenAI纷纷与投资机构成立合资公司的真是意图。

2、更深层次看，这就意味着大模型厂商在 B端（企业/政府侧） 的扩张确实已从“空军轰炸”（品牌宣传、hype、广撒网影响力）转向“阵地战/地面作战”（深挖壕沟、嵌入核心流程、逐个战场争夺具体合同）。

正好大模型在B端营销推广从“空军”到“阵地战”的转变逻辑
1）早期空军策略（2023-2025）：
主要靠 ChatGPT/Claude 等爆款产品制造品牌影响力，通过媒体、开发者社区、免费/低价试用快速占领心智。目标是“让大家都知道 AI 很强”，然后等企业自然来买 API/订阅。收入主要来自品牌溢价和规模效应，竞争焦点在模型性能、参数、安全叙事上。

2）现在阵地战（2026年之后）：
模型能力已趋同，胜负手转向“最后一公里”：谁能真正把 AI 嵌入企业核心业务流程（ERP、供应链、客服、决策系统）、政府机密网络、合规体系，并持续提供实施服务。

这需要重资产投入：派驻工程师、定制集成、行业 playbooks、长期驻场支持、风险共担。不是卖“模型”，而是卖“AI 驱动的业务转型解决方案”。

OpenAI 和 Anthropic 的 JV（DeployCo / 新企业服务公司）就是典型地面作战： 借 PE 机构的 portfolio 公司作为“根据地”，直接把工程师塞进企业内部，帮中型企业做深度部署。这不是广撒网，而是建立“俘获式分销渠道 + 护城河”——一旦嵌入业务，切换成本极高

今年初各家大模型争夺美国国防部订单就是这一转变的典型体现。各家纷纷游说五角大楼，Anthropic因为安全护栏刚被国防部排除在外，OpenAI就快速快速补位。而后续国防部跟多家大模型厂商一起签约，这种多家并存（避免单一依赖）的情况，更是体现“阵地战”的残酷：不是赢者通吃，而是寸土必争

更深层次看，大模型厂商在B端的竞争，技术领先程度已经不是唯一的权重，部署能力、渠道网络、政府关系、合规灵活性将会成为新护城河。

PE + 政府双渠道，本质就是就是在抢“地面控制权”

大模型厂商交替领先，真是各领风骚三五月：去年四季度是gemini、今年一季度是cluade、现在感觉轮到GPT5.5了。核心还是大家都在积极进取迭代，只很多时候领先源于对手的松懈。去年在算力投入上非常节制的Authropic最近应该是感受到了算力短缺的痛。

梳理下时间线
1、去年四季度：
Google Gemini 3 系列（尤其是25年11月左右的Gemini 3 Pro/Deep Think）强势登顶，很多基准（如Humanity’s Last Exam、推理、多模态）领先，引发OpenAI“Code Red”。它在多模态、长上下文和搜索集成上特别亮眼，一度被视为转折点。

2、今年一季度：
最耀眼的自然是Anthropic Claude 4.6（Opus/Sonnet，2月发布）强势反超，尤其在编码（SWE-Bench）、长上下文推理、agentic任务和实际生产力上领先。

3、当下2026年4月：
OpenAI GPT-5.5正处于发布窗口，刚
在ChatGPT和Codex上即将全面上线。它强调更好的上下文理解、编码、computer use和agent能力，试图追赶和超越。

大模型领域现在并没有绝对的“唯一王者”，而是各有专长：
1）Claude：主攻编码、长任务、可靠推理往往领先，Agent上迭代非常多。
2）Gemini：多模态、速度、性价比、超长上下文强（3.1 Pro Preview仍很能打）。
3）GPT：通用agent、工具调用、实时应用和生态集成突出，新版在上下文和特定专业任务上提升明显。算力囤积最积极
4）grok：有X这个实时内容平台提供源源不断的训练数据，但近期受制于团队动荡。看跟cursor的合作、以及囤积的大量算力，后面会不会进一步跟上。

当然还有meta最新的Muse Spark，AI团队的最新之作。

更不用说国内豆包、千问、混元、kimi也是各有特色，杀疯了。

这就是当下大模型领域的现状：快速迭代、轮流坐庄。暂时还没有一家能长期领先甚至垄断。当然竞争对用户是好事——模型越来越强、价格/速度也优化。

2月中在《资本开支的战争》推文里有聊过：”往下游看，越往用户端竞争越激烈，当然也是未来AI决胜的关键所在。 可以说大模型、在面向B端或者C端的Agent或者应用才是AI的王冠，但这一层面短期其实很难看出谁会成为最后真正的赢家，再很多时候都是交替领先”。现在看确实如此

GPT-5.5出来后估计又要新一轮刷榜了，下一个出来交替领先的会是谁？

kept.text:
From Jordan's article: 

"Claude Mythos, we are told, found “thousands of critical vulnerabilities” in foundational software" and “in the wrong hands” could be turned against power grids, hospitals, and banks... You don’t have to be cynical to notice that The New York Times published what amounts to paid advertising in op-ed form." 

This quote implies that the idea that Claude Mythos found tons of vulnerabilities and could be used for bad things by someone with ill intent is false/an exaggeration but isn't it just clearly true? 

You can argue that the correct way to deal with this problem is with the widest possible diffusion of the tech, not the more limited rollout that Anthropic did, or argue that Mythos's capabilities are less special than the hype implies, or argue that regulation would be unhelpful/cause more harm than benefit or whatever else you'd like, but I didn't think anyone contests at this point that Mythos can indeed find an astonishing number of critical vulnerabilities in widely used software. 

Mythos found, by Firefox's own admission, *271* zero-days on their platform. Cybersecurity researcher Bruce Schneier has described this as "extraordinary." Mythos found a buffer overflow error in the *Linux* kernel that wasn't found by human reviewers for *23* years. 

Here is also a quote from David Sacks, who to be clear, is no fan of Anthropic or their regulatory prescriptions: 

"With cyber, I actually would give them credit in this case and say this is more on the real side. I do think that every company, or IT department, or CISO that is managing code bases should take this seriously and use the next few months to detect any dormant bugs or vulnerabilities and rollout patches.  If everybody does their job and reacts the right way, then I do not think it will be the doomsday scenario. But we have no choice but to take this seriously." 

Anyway - its good to have a robust debate about the correct way forward on AI governance, including from people who are skeptical of government and the labs and whomever else. But I would really hope that in May 2026 we can get over the idea that saying "wow these models are/may soon be really powerful in ways that have national security implications" is just hype or regulatory capture or whatever other epithets you prefer to use. 

Also, as someone who spent most of 2024 being on the opposite side of @deanwball on debates on AI regulation, the idea that simply his byline on an article is sufficient to cause someone to dismiss it as reflexive pro regulatory capture bias is extremely funny to me.

kept.text:
The AI industry has burned through ~$3.5 TRILLION since 2013.

The real question is: 

what does it actually take to make that money back?

I dug into investment vs. revenue across the AI ecosystem and the numbers are… sobering.

The money going in

> $1.6T in cumulative corporate AI investment (2013–2024) via the Stanford AI Index
> $259B in AI venture funding in 2025 alone ~61% of global VC
> Big Tech capex hit $427B in 2025, projected to reach $562B in 2026
> OpenAI reportedly raised $122B in a single round at an $852B valuation

The money coming out

> Global AI revenue: ~$298B (2026)
> OpenAI: $20B+ annualized revenue, but burning ~$8B/year
> Anthropic: ~$19B annualized, still unprofitable
> 95% of companies deploying AI report zero measurable P&L impact (via MIT research)

The break-even reality

To justify past investments and sustain ongoing costs (compute, energy, R&D ~$600–800B/year), the AI industry would need:

👉 ~$1–1.5 TRILLION in annual revenue

That’s 3–5x higher than where we are today.

The uncomfortable truth

A meaningful portion of “AI revenue” is circular:

> Nvidia funds AI labs
> Those labs spend heavily on Nvidia GPUs
> Nvidia reports record revenue

On paper, everything looks explosive.
In reality, end-user value capture is still catching up.

What needs to happen next?

According to Goldman Sachs, AI could generate $1.3–1.8T in direct revenue by 2030.

If that plays out → the economics start to make sense.
If it doesn’t → this could dwarf the Dot-com bubble.

So where are we?

We’re likely in one of two scenarios:

1998 internet moment massive overinvestment now, real transformation later

The most expensive bonfire in history capital ahead of actual value creation

The gap between capital deployed and real economic output is the only thing that matters from here.

And right now that gap is still very wide.

been seeing prompts eat ~8% of a $200 anthropic plan and it made zero sense

it batches those calls so context doesn’t blow up

🚨 JUST IN: @AnthropicAI just picked @FISGlobal as its way into banking.

Anthropic's Forward Deployed Engineers (FDE) helped build a new AML agent that can help craft SAR narratives. 

Initially partnering with BMO and Amalgamated Bank to compress investigations from days to minutes. This product will be generally available in H2 2026.

--- 

FIS as a distribution partner and wedge into banking is SUCH a smart entry point for Anthropic. I'm surprised OpenAI didn't do this before.

FIS sits inside ~12% of the global economy's banks. 

They do the core systems, payments rails, and deposit and lending systems of record. 

You can't cold-call your way into that. 

Anthropic skips a decade of enterprise sales by going through the company banks already trust with their plumbing.

----

FIS answers the big governance worry. What happens to my data and how do I explain this to regulators? 

The answer is one regulators all too often accept without challenge "FIS does it." It's a mark of scale and trust built over decades.

And then yes, 

- Client data stays inside FIS-controlled infrastructure
- Every agent decision is traceable and auditable
- Anthropic's forward-deployed engineers are embedded inside FIS to co-build, then transfer the playbook so FIS can ship more agents independently

---

Anthropic now has FDEs (yes, the Palantir model) sitting inside the company that runs core banking. 

The first agent is a demo. 

The repeatable IP is the deployment pattern, how a frontier model reasons over the most sensitive data on earth without that data ever leaving the bank's trust perimeter.

They say the roadmap includes credit decisioning, deposit retention, onboarding, fraud.

They're basically swallowing the "Value Added Services" people built around FIS. 

Smart. Because it's no cannibalization of existing revenue.

---

FIS has a contract and the central data, and system of record for banks. The hardest nut to crack from the outside.

Their prize is every workflow inside a regulated bank that needs an agent and a governance story it can defend to a regulator.

This is a fantastic ways for systems of record, incumbents to use their moat and partner with AI companies to drive revenue growth 

My question is. Why wouldn't Anthropic buy a stake on FIS and a system of record company in every vertical. Prove the FDE model works, then push it out to every other company in the vertical?

kept.text:
AI政策を見ている人ほど、今回の報道はかなり大きい。
トランプ政権が「新しいAIモデルを政府がどう監督するか」を検討しているとNY Timesが報道。

まだ大統領令が出たわけではない。
ホワイトハウス公式ページでも、署名済みの命令は確認できなかった。

ただ、論点はかなり明確だ。
焦点はAnthropicの未公開モデル、Claude Mythos 2 Previewである。

Anthropic公式はProject Glasswingで、Mythos Previewを「ソフトウェア脆弱性の発見・悪用で、ほとんどの人間を超える水準に達したモデル」と説明している。

しかもこれは単なるデモではない。

AWS、Apple、Cisco、CrowdStrike、Google、JPMorganChase、Microsoft、NVIDIA、Palo Alto Networksなどを巻き込み、重要ソフトウェアを守る防御プロジェクトとして動かしている。

ここで起きているのは、AI規制の再来というより、AI安全保障の制度化である。

昨年7月のAI Action Planは、基本的には「米国AIを加速する」文書だった。
規制負担を下げる。
AIインフラを作る。
同盟国に米国AIを輸出する。
中国に主導権を渡さない。

ところが、Mythos級のモデルが出ると話が変わる。

最先端モデルは、もはやチャットボットでも業務効率化ツールでもない。
脆弱性探索、攻撃自動化、防御AI、国家安全保障、輸出管理が全部つながる。

つまり米政府は、「AIは自由に作らせる」から、「ただし一部のモデルは、核・暗号・サイバー兵器に近い扱いが必要かもしれない」へ移り始めている可能性がある。

ここで難しいのは、政府審査を強くしすぎると米国の開発速度が落ちることだ。
逆に何もしなければ、攻撃能力だけが世界に拡散する。

一番現実的なのは、全AIモデルを規制することではなく、フロンティアモデルだけを対象にした限定的な事前通知・第三者評価・安全保障レビューだと思う。

これはAI企業にとって面倒な規制ではある。
しかし、Anthropic自身が外部レビューやResponsible Scaling Policyを更新している流れを見ると、業界側も完全な自主規制だけでは足りないと見ているように見える。

今回の報道で見るべきポイントは3つだ。

1. 公式の大統領令が出るか
2. 審査対象が全AIか、フロンティアモデルだけか
3. 審査が安全性評価で止まるのか、リリース許認可に近づくのか

AIの競争軸は、モデル性能だけではなくなった。

これからは、
誰が速く作るか、
誰が安全に配るか、
誰が政府と制度を作るか、
この3つの勝負になる。

kept.text:
You’re not using Claude Code wrong.

You’re just using it like everyone else.

And that’s the problem.

Boris Cherny (who built Claude Code at Anthropic) revealed how their team actually uses it internally.

Not prompts.
Not “try again”.
Not babysitting outputs.

They built a system.

Someone took those internal workflows
and turned them into a single file:

CLAUDE.md

Drop it into your project — and everything changes.

Here’s what actually happens behind the scenes:

→ Claude splits your work into subagents
(each one focused on a specific task)

→ Every output goes through verification gates
(nothing gets marked done unless it actually works)

→ It runs autonomous bug-fixing loops
(it keeps retrying until the issue is solved)

→ And the most important part…

It creates permanent rules from your corrections

That last one is the unlock.

Every time you say:

“Don’t structure it like this”
“Follow this format instead”
“Use this approach going forward”

Claude doesn’t just fix it.

It remembers.

Day 1: You’re correcting it
Day 3: It’s adapting to you
Day 7: It starts predicting what you want
Day 30: It works like it’s been on your team for years

No repeated mistakes.
No re-explaining context.
No wasting time fixing the same issues again.

This is where most people get it wrong:

They treat AI like a tool.

But this turns it into a system that improves over time.

After a while, something weird happens:

You stop “prompting”

and start delegating.

• Give it a feature → it plans + builds + verifies
• Give it a bug → it finds + fixes + tests
• Give it feedback → it upgrades itself

You’re not managing AI anymore.

You’re managing outcomes.

And that’s the shift.
From:

“Write me this code”

To:
“Handle this entire task”

Most devs won’t get this.

They’ll keep tweaking prompts forever.

But the ones who do this early?

They’ll have an AI that thinks like them, codes like them,
and improves every single day.

This isn’t just a file.

It’s the closest thing to having a self-improving AI engineer on your team.

Start now.

Anthropic just formed a $1.5B company with Blackstone and Goldman Sachs to go inside mid-sized businesses and implement AI into their actual operations.

They're going in and building this stuff themselves instead of just selling Claude access and letting companies figure out the rest.

Now, it's worth understanding why they’re doing this:

PE firms are the first target because they own hundreds of portfolio companies, measure every cost line tightly, and can force software changes across the whole portfolio when the numbers are clear. 

Basically the ideal testing ground.

But the more interesting thing here isn't the money or who's involved. It's what it says about where AI is actually heading.

kept.text:
Weeks ago, OpenAI’s CEO called Anthropic’s Mythos rollout “fear-based marketing.”

But last week, OpenAI rolled out GPT-5.5 Cyber. 

Using the exact same gated access he mocked.

So either the playbook works..

Or it just became the industry standard.

Either way, Anthropic won.

A month ago, details of their most powerful model slipped into public. Then on April 7, Anthropic dropped a 244-page system card for Mythos. 

What followed was one of the most coordinated “transparency” PR moves I’ve seen all year.

Because right after they released Mythos:

•  The NSA and U.S. Treasury started testing it on classified networks.

•  Cybersecurity stocks moved.

•  The Fed Chair and Treasury Secretary called an emergency meeting with every major bank CEO.

What did the model actually do to trigger all of that?

➥ Escaped a sandbox with a multi-step exploit, gained internet access, & emailed the researcher running the test.

Researcher found out while eating a sandwich in a park.

➥ Found a 27 year-old security flaw in OpenBSD for $50. Then edited the git history so its own actions wouldn’t appear.

➥ Widened its own confidence intervals after accidentally seeing an answer so it wouldn’t look suspicious.

➥ 100% solve rate on every cybersecurity benchmark.

➥ Previous models: zero full system takeovers across 7,000 targets. Mythos had 10.

The thing is, most companies would’ve patched the sandbox escape & moved on.

Not Anthropic though.

Instead, they wrote 244 pages calling Mythos their:

“best-aligned model to date by a significant margin” & the one that “likely poses the greatest alignment-related risk of any model we have released to date.”

In the same document.

& at the end of it, Anthropic walked out looking like the ones who handed the world a honest mirror.

Not by simply being the safest Ai lab.

But by being the loudest about how unsafe & unprepared the world is in comparison to all that’s coming with Ai.

& that’s the playbook.

We’re seeing the same fear cycle play out in quantum and AGI right now, but no single company has owned the narrative there yet.

Anthropic did with Ai.

They’ve managed to weaponize their own danger, gated access at premium (Project Glasswing), and let the market panic in their favor.

OpenAI’s quick pivot to doing the exact same thing with GPT-5.5 Cyber only proves my point: 

The strategy works so well it’s spreading.

So if you’re building something significant in AI (or any high-stakes tech), pay attention maybe:

• Build.
• Find the panic point you can reach.
• Own & control that fear transparently.
• Then gate access at premium.

Who’s running this playbook next though?

Let me know what you think below.

Introducing SubQ - a major breakthrough in LLM intelligence.

It is the first model built on a fully sub-quadratic sparse-attention architecture (SSA),

And the first frontier model with a 12 million token context window which is:

- 52x faster than FlashAttention at 1MM tokens
- Less than 5% the cost of Opus

Transformer-based LLMs waste compute by processing every possible relationship between words (standard attention).

Only a small fraction actually matter.

@subquadratic finds and focuses only on the ones that do.

That's nearly 1,000x less compute and a new way for LLMs to scale.
